revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:伊福部氏]]
'''Iokibe Clan''' (伊福部氏, ''Iokibe-uji'') is a [[Japanese clans|Japanese clan]] whose family name originates from the [[Iokibe]] region. The clan is also referred to as '''Iokikibe Clan''' (伊福吉部氏), '''Iokibe Clan''' (五百木部氏), and '''Iobe Clan''' (廬城部氏).
== Overview ==
The "Iokibe no Omi Clan" was the [[Tomonomiyatsuko|Tomo no Miyatsuko]] of the [[Iokibe]] region in {{ill|Inaba Province|en|Inaba Province|qid=Q1054338|lt=Inaba Province|1=Inaba Province}}. According to the genealogical record ''Inaba no Kuni Iokibe no Omi Kogi'' compiled during the late {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|qid=Q189178|lt=Nara period|1=Nara period}}, the clan is identified as a branch of the [[Mononobe clan|Mononobe Clan]]. The ''{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|en|Shinsen Shōjiroku|qid=Q1045362|lt=Shinsen Shōjiroku|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku}}'' lists "Iokibe Sukunoe Clan" and "Iokibe Muraji Clan" under "Sakyō Divine Descent," describing them as descendants of the {{ill|Owari clan|en|Owari clan|qid=Q11465311|lt=Owari clan|1=Owari clan}} and the god [[Amenokaguyama-no-Mikoto]]. The same text lists Iokibe clans under various regional categories, indicating a widespread influence.
The Iokibe clan held Ranks such as [[Omi (kabane)|Omi]] and [[Muraji]] while including branches with titles like [[Tomi (kabane)|Tomi]] and [[Kimi (kabane)|Kimi]]. Some family members were granted lower titles like [[Naori (kabane)|Naori]] and [[Kubi (kabane)|Kubi]].
According to ''Inaba no Kuni Iokibe no Omi Kogi'' (circa {{ill|Enryaku|en|Enryaku|qid=Q1373176|lt=Enryaku|1=Enryaku}} 3), the clan descends from the legendary god {{ill|Ikagashiko-o|ja|伊香色雄|1=Ikagashiko-o|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikagashiko-o|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikagashiko-o命|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q55536799}} and his son Takemuguchi-no-Mikoto. The clan's ancestor is said to have accompanied {{ill|Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto|en|Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto|qid=Q65318|lt=Prince Takeiwatatsu|1=Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto}} to subdue bandits in {{ill|Inaba Province|en|Inaba Province|qid=Q1054338|lt=Inaba Province|1=Inaba Province}} during the westward campaign of {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊|1=Yamato Takeru|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|3=ヤマトタケル|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ヤマトタケル|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q461258}}. The clan later received the title of {{ill|Inaba no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|稲葉国造|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inaba no Kuni no Miyatsuko|1=Inaba Kuni no Miyatsuko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q85882968}} during Emperor Seimu's reign. Another tradition claims that during Emperor Ingyō's time, a clan member transformed into a wind deity and received the surname ''Kifukibe-no-Omi''.
The 25th head, Kuzura, is noted for his involvement in compiling clan records during the reign of Empress Suiko and under the direction of {{ill|Prince Shōtoku|en|Prince Shōtoku|qid=Q263972|lt=Shōtoku Taishi|1=Prince Shōtoku}} and {{ill|Soga no Umako|en|Soga no Umako|qid=Q1191233|lt=Soga no Umako|1=Soga no Umako}}.
The 26th head, Tumushi (都牟自), served as the first governor of {{ill|Takahisa District|ja|高草郡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takahisa District|1=Takakusa district|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11672760}} during Emperor Kōtoku's reign. He implemented administrative reforms and passed away on {{ill|April 18|en|April 18|qid=Q2521|lt=April 18|1=April 18}}, {{ill|658|en|658|qid=Q30176|lt=658|1=658}}.
A notable female clan member, {{ill|Ifukube_no_Tokotari|en|Ifukube_no_Tokotari|qid=Q11379699|lt=Ifukube no Tokotari|1=Ifukube no Tokotari}}, served as a {{ill|Uneme|lt=Uneme|lt=Saimei no Tennō|ja|采女|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saimei no Tennō|qq=}} during Emperor Monmu's reign. She passed away in {{ill|708|en|708|qid=Q23511|lt=708|1=708}}, leaving behind a bronze funerary urn inscribed with one of Japan's oldest extant inscriptions.
== Notable Events == * A clan member, {{ill|Iobe no Muraji Kikoyu|ja|廬城部連枳莒喩|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iobe no Muraji Kikoyu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, served during the reigns of Emperors Yūryaku and Ankan but caused scandals involving royal property. * In {{ill|684|en|684|qid=Q31128|lt=684|1=684}}, during Emperor Tenmu's reign, the clan received the {{ill|kabane|ja|八色の姓|1=Eight Kabane system|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kabane|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Kabane system|qq=}} Rank of [[Sukune]], reflecting their high status.
== Cultural Artifacts == * The ''Iokibe Gate'' (殷富門) in the imperial palace commemorates the clan's involvement in eliminating Soga no Iruka.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Sources == * ''Nihon Shoki (Volume 3)'', [[Iwanami Bunko]], 1994. * ''Nihon Shoki: Complete Modern Translation (Volume 1)'', [[Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko]], translated by {{ill|Takeshi Ujiyasu|ja|宇治谷孟|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeshi Ujiyasu|1=Ujitani Mitsuru|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11449517}}, 1988. * ''Shoku Nihongi Volume 2,'' New [[Nihon Koten Bungaku Taikei]] 13, {{ill|Iwanami Shoten|en|Iwanami Shoten|qid=Q1331427|lt=Iwanami Shoten|1=Iwanami Shoten}}, 1990. * ''Iwanami Dictionary of Japanese History,'' p. 84, {{ill|Iwanami Shoten|en|Iwanami Shoten|qid=Q1331427|lt=Iwanami Shoten|1=Iwanami Shoten}}, 1999, supervised by {{ill|Keiji Nagahara|ja|永原慶二|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Keiji Nagahara|1=Keiji Nagahara|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11549910}}. * ''Japanese Ancient Clan Dictionary,'' [[Yuzankaku]], revised edition 2015, edited by {{ill|Arikiyo Saeki|ja|佐伯有清|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arikiyo Saeki|1=Saeki Ariyoshi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q13364959}}.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Inaba Clan|ja|因幡氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inaba Clan|1=Inaba clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11420261}} * {{ill|Uneme|lt=Uneme|lt=Saimei no Tennō|ja|采女|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saimei no Tennō|qq=}}
== External Links == * [http://houki.yonago-kodaisi.com/F-BHYS-iokibesi.html ''Iokibe Clan'']
{{Japanese-history-stub}} {{translated page|ja|伊福部氏|version=100476552|comment=Imported full ja history}}
